name: Nintendo Wii
short_description: A home game console whose motion-sensing Wii Remote made physical gesture the primary input, drawing families and non-gamers into shared play.
long_description: Released in November 2006, the Nintendo Wii deliberately broke from the console industry's race for raw processing power and instead invested in a novel physical interface: a handheld Wii Remote that detected three-axis acceleration and pointed at the screen via infrared sensors. The bundled Wii Sports — bowling, tennis, golf, baseball, boxing — made the interaction immediately legible to anyone who had swung an arm, and the console became a landmark multi-user device: up to four players with simultaneous remotes, full-household generational range, and a living-room social dynamic that had more in common with board game night than solo gaming. Selling 101.63 million units, the Wii demonstrated that changing the physical form of the input — from button matrix to embodied gesture — could redefine who a device is for and how many people participate at once.
ai_note: The Wii Remote uses accelerometers and infrared point-tracking for gesture input; no machine-learning or AI runs onboard — game responses are rule-based physics simulations.

images.caption: Nintendo Wii console with Wii Remote and Nunchuk controller.
images.alt_text: A white Nintendo Wii console standing vertically next to a white Wii Remote controller and its Nunchuk accessory, all against a plain white background.

sources.title: Wii — Wikipedia
sources.access_notes: Launch date (November 19, 2006 North America), sales figures (101.63 million), Wii Sports bundle, Wii Remote technical specs, design philosophy from Satoru Iwata.

sources.title: The Social Living Room: How Nintendo's Wii Revolutionized Gaming
sources.access_notes: Analysis of the Wii's multi-user, living-room social gaming design and its legacy.

sources.title: Wii Remote — Wikipedia
sources.access_notes: Technical details: three-axis accelerometer, infrared sensor, Bluetooth connectivity, up to four simultaneous remotes.

interactions.title: Swing your arm to bowl or serve
interactions.description: In Wii Sports, players stand up and mimic the real motion — drawing the Wii Remote back and releasing to roll a bowling ball, or swinging it like a racket to return a tennis serve.

interactions.title: Box with both fists
interactions.description: The boxing game pairs the Wii Remote with the Nunchuk so each hand controls one of the on-screen fighter's gloves; players throw left and right punches in the air to land hits.

interactions.title: Step onto the Balance Board for yoga and strength training
interactions.description: Wii Fit (2007) added the Balance Board peripheral that tracks the player's center of gravity and weight; users hold tree pose, do push-ups and lunges, hula-hoop, and ski down virtual slopes by shifting their balance.

interactions.title: Four remotes, one couch
interactions.description: Up to four players pair Wii Remotes at once over Bluetooth, so a whole household can take turns or compete together in the same room rather than online.

interactions.title: Wiihabilitation in clinics and nursing homes
interactions.description: Therapists adopted Wii Sports and Wii Fit for rehab, having stroke survivors and post-surgery patients play bowling or balance games to rebuild range of motion, strength, and motor control — work clinicians nicknamed 'Wiihabilitation.'

interactions.title: Turn the remote into a $5 interactive whiteboard and head-tracked 3D display
interactions.description: Researcher Johnny Chung Lee exploited the Wii Remote's infrared camera and built-in blob tracking: an IR pen turns any projector surface into a touch whiteboard, and an IR sensor on the head makes a TV render head-tracked 3D perspective. His software was downloaded hundreds of thousands of times.

interactions.title: Conduct music or paint in the air
interactions.description: Because the remote streams three-axis acceleration and pointer position, a creative tool could let someone wave it like a baton to shape tempo and dynamics, or sweep it across the screen to paint — extending the same gesture sensing the sports games already used.

interactions.title: Co-located physical therapy duet
interactions.description: Pairing the Balance Board with a second remote, a patient and a caregiver could mirror each other's movements in a shared balance game, turning solo rehab exercises into a two-person, in-the-room routine.
